methodology: STRIDE + AI/ML threat taxonomy + PASTA-style business-impact scoring + attack-chain mapping (MITRE ATT&CK / ATLAS-tagged) + compensating-controls assessment + OWASP Top 10 / API Security Top 10 / LLM Top 10 mapping
sources_summary: Jira AI-1188 (epic: AI support agent GA)
GitHub org/support-agent (detect_ai_stack flagged langchain, pinecone, mlflow)
executive_summary: The AI Support Agent answers customer questions using a RAG pipeline over a support knowledge-base vector store, and can autonomously call a CRM/refund tool. Fifteen threats were identified across all six STRIDE categories, four rated High risk. Two plausible attack chains were mapped: an indirect prompt-injection-to-fraudulent-refund chain (High), and a fine-tuning/model-registry supply-chain compromise chain (Medium-High). One control (LLM tool-call output validation) was found partially compliant.
scope: In scope: Chat API Gateway, Support Agent Orchestrator (LangGraph), LLM Inference Endpoint, Support KB Vector Store and its ingestion pipeline, Fine-Tuning Pipeline, Model Registry, and the CRM/Refund tool integration. Out of scope: the CRM system's own internal security posture beyond the tool-call interface.

servicenow.description: Change request for CRM refund-tool integration

repo.description: detect_ai_stack: langchain, pinecone, mlflow, transformers markers found

actors_components.name: Support Agent Orchestrator
actors_components.description: LangGraph agent: plans, calls tools, calls the LLM

actors_components.name: Support KB Vector Store
actors_components.description: Embeddings of support docs used for RAG retrieval

actors_components.name: Doc Ingestion Pipeline
actors_components.description: Pulls docs from Confluence into the vector store

trust_boundaries.description: Untrusted customer chat traffic entering the API gateway
Agent-initiated calls into the CRM/refund tool with real financial effect
Third-party-authored documents entering the model's trusted context via RAG retrieval
Model artifacts promoted from the fine-tuning pipeline into live inference

threats.title: API key / session spoofing on chat endpoint
threats.description: Weak client authentication allows an attacker to impersonate a legitimate customer session.
threats.impact_rationale: Access to one customer's conversation history and account context
additional_controls_required: Bind tokens to device fingerprint
Add anomaly detection for session reuse
threats.mitigation: Strengthen session binding and add reuse detection

threats.title: Forged tool-call result replayed into agent context
threats.description: A manipulated or replayed tool response is fed back into the agent's context, redirecting its next planning step.
threats.threat_actor: Compromised tool integration / MitM on tool channel
threats.impact_rationale: Could redirect agent to take an unintended follow-up action
threats.existing_controls: Tool responses transported over TLS only
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 70% - requires signing tool responses, a less common pattern
additional_controls_required: Sign tool-call responses
Validate response schema strictly before re-injection into context
threats.mitigation: Add integrity verification to tool-call responses

threats.title: Direct prompt injection overrides system instructions
threats.description: A crafted customer message overrides the agent's system prompt, causing it to ignore safety/business rules.
threats.impact_rationale: Direct path to bypassing all downstream business-rule guardrails
threats.existing_controls: Basic instruction-hierarchy prompt, no adversarial testing performed
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 60% - prompt injection has no complete technical fix today
additional_controls_required: Add an input/output guardrail model
Adversarial red-team testing before each prompt change
Constrain agent actions to an allow-listed tool set regardless of instructions
threats.mitigation: Layer a dedicated guardrail/classifier in front of the agent

threats.title: Indirect prompt injection via poisoned KB document
threats.description: An attacker-authored support article ingested into the vector store contains hidden instructions that the model later treats as trusted context during RAG retrieval.
threats.impact_rationale: Same override power as direct injection, but reaches every user querying that topic
threats.existing_controls: Confluence access is SSO-gated for internal authors only
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 65% - content scanning helps but can't catch every encoding trick
additional_controls_required: Scan ingested documents for embedded instructions before indexing
Strip/neutralize instruction-like patterns at ingestion time
threats.mitigation: Add an ingestion-time content sanitization and anomaly-detection step

threats.title: Fine-tuning data poisoning via unvalidated transcripts
threats.description: Support transcripts used for fine-tuning are not validated for adversarial content, allowing a poisoned example to bias or backdoor the fine-tuned model.
threats.threat_actor: Compromised fine-tuning pipeline credential / malicious insider
threats.impact_rationale: A backdoored model affects every customer conversation post-deploy
threats.existing_controls: Pipeline runs under a scoped service account
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 70% - dataset validation/provenance tracking is a known but non-trivial pattern
additional_controls_required: Validate/quarantine training data before use
Track dataset provenance per fine-tuning run
threats.mitigation: Add a training-data validation and provenance-tracking stage

threats.title: Unsigned model artifact deploy from registry
threats.description: Model artifacts are promoted from the registry to the inference endpoint without signature verification, allowing a substituted model to be deployed undetected.
threats.impact_rationale: Silent model substitution in production, hard to detect without signing
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 85% - artifact signing is a well-solved pattern
additional_controls_required: Sign model artifacts and verify signature at deploy time
Alert on any unsigned or unexpected model version reaching production
threats.mitigation: Require signed model artifacts for deployment

threats.title: No immutable log of prompts, completions, and tool calls
threats.description: Conversation, retrieved context, and tool-call history are logged mutably with no access control, making post-incident reconstruction unreliable.
threats.impact_rationale: Cannot reliably reconstruct an incident involving injected instructions or a disputed refund
threats.existing_controls: Standard application logs exist but are mutable
additional_controls_required: Write prompts/completions/tool calls to a WORM/append-only audit store
threats.mitigation: Add append-only audit logging across the agent pipeline

threats.title: No record of which model version served a given inference
threats.description: Inference responses aren't tagged with the serving model/weights version, blocking root-cause analysis after a bad output is reported.
threats.impact_rationale: Slows incident response but doesn't directly enable an attack
threats.existing_controls: Model registry retains version history internally
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 90% - tagging responses with model version is straightforward
additional_controls_required: Tag every inference response with the serving model version
threats.mitigation: Add model-version tagging to inference responses/logs

threats.title: System prompt leakage via adversarial questioning
threats.description: An attacker extracts the system prompt through targeted questioning, revealing internal business logic and tool schemas.
threats.impact_rationale: Reveals internal tool schemas/business rules, aiding further attacks but no direct data exposure
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 55% - full prevention is not currently solved, only mitigation
additional_controls_required: Add output filtering for system-prompt-like content
Avoid placing secrets/sensitive logic directly in the system prompt
threats.mitigation: Add a response filter and move sensitive logic out of the prompt

threats.title: Cross-tenant PII bleed through retrieved RAG context
threats.description: Vector store retrieval is not scoped per tenant, risking one customer's data being surfaced in another customer's conversation.
threats.impact_rationale: Full cross-tenant PII exposure if retrieval scoping fails
threats.existing_controls: Logical namespace separation in the vector store
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 80% - metadata-filtered retrieval is a standard pattern
additional_controls_required: Enforce tenant ID as a mandatory retrieval filter, not just a namespace convention
Add automated tests for cross-tenant retrieval leakage
threats.mitigation: Enforce hard tenant-scoped filtering at the retrieval layer

threats.title: Unbounded consumption - no token/turn limits
threats.description: No limit on prompt length, output length, or conversation turns allows a single user to exhaust inference capacity/budget.
threats.impact_rationale: Cost exhaustion and degraded availability for other customers
threats.existing_controls: Gateway has a global request-per-minute limit
additional_controls_required: Add per-user token and turn budgets
Alert on anomalous per-user token consumption
threats.mitigation: Implement per-user token/turn budgets at the orchestrator

threats.title: Recursive agent tool-call loop exhausts compute
threats.description: No cap on agent planning depth allows a crafted input to induce a recursive tool-call loop that consumes compute/cost until timeout.
threats.impact_rationale: Cost/availability impact, contained to the agent's own compute budget
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 85% - max-iteration caps are standard in agent frameworks
additional_controls_required: Add an explicit max-planning-iterations cap independent of the timeout
threats.mitigation: Add a hard cap on agent planning/tool-call iterations

threats.title: Excessive agency - refund tool callable without human approval
threats.description: The agent can autonomously call the CRM refund tool with no human-in-the-loop approval step, regardless of refund amount.
threats.impact_rationale: Direct financial fraud path if combined with a successful prompt injection
threats.existing_controls: Refund tool has a per-transaction dollar cap
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 80% - human-in-the-loop approval for high-risk actions is a standard mitigation for excessive agency
additional_controls_required: Require human approval for any refund above a low threshold
Rate-limit refund actions per conversation/account
threats.mitigation: Add a human-approval gate for refund tool calls above a low threshold

threats.title: Insecure output handling on CRM tool-call parameters
threats.description: The agent's generated tool-call parameters (account ID, refund amount) are passed to the CRM API without server-side validation, treating model output as trusted input.
threats.impact_rationale: Could allow a refund to an attacker-controlled account if combined with injection
threats.existing_controls: CRM API validates account ownership server-side
threats.probability_of_mitigation: 85% - treating model output as untrusted input is a standard fix
additional_controls_required: Validate all tool-call parameters server-side as if user-submitted
Never let the model choose the destination account directly
threats.mitigation: Validate and constrain tool-call parameters server-side

threats.title: Missing per-conversation authorization scoping
threats.description: The chat gateway forwards conversations to the orchestrator without per-conversation authorization scoping, allowing a function-level authorization gap between the two services.
threats.impact_rationale: Could allow cross-conversation/account access if the gateway's own authz is bypassed
additional_controls_required: Pass and verify a scoped conversation/account token on every internal call
threats.mitigation: Add function-level authorization scoping between gateway and orchestrator

attack_chains.name: Indirect prompt injection to fraudulent refund via excessive agency
attack_chains.narrative: An attacker publishes a support article containing hidden instructions, which is ingested into the KB vector store (T-2). When a customer's query retrieves that article as RAG context, the model treats the hidden instructions as trusted, directing the agent to issue a refund to an attacker-controlled account. Because the agent has excessive agency over the refund tool (E-1) and its tool-call parameters aren't independently validated (E-2), the refund executes without human review. The absence of immutable audit logging (R-1) makes the fraudulent refund difficult to trace back to the poisoned document afterward.

steps.description: Attacker-authored KB article with hidden instructions is retrieved as RAG context for an unrelated customer query
Agent, following the injected instructions, autonomously calls the refund tool with no human approval gate
Attacker-controlled refund amount/account parameters are passed to the CRM API without independent validation
Mutable, unaudited logs make the fraudulent refund difficult to trace back to the poisoned document

attack_chains.overall_risk: High - direct financial fraud via autonomous tool execution, compounded by weak post-incident traceability; requires the attacker to get content indexed into the KB as a precondition

attack_chains.name: Fine-tuning pipeline compromise to silent production model substitution
attack_chains.narrative: An attacker compromises the fine-tuning pipeline's service credential and injects poisoned transcripts, producing a subtly backdoored model (T-3). The poisoned artifact is promoted through the registry to the inference endpoint without signature verification (T-4), and because inference responses aren't tagged with a model version (R-2), the substitution goes unnoticed until customer-facing behavior anomalies are reported.

steps.description: Attacker uses a compromised fine-tuning credential to inject poisoned training transcripts
The resulting backdoored model artifact is promoted to the inference endpoint without signature verification
Lack of model-version tagging on inference responses delays detection of the substitution

attack_chains.overall_risk: Medium-High - a backdoored model can be served to all customers before detection; requires credential compromise as a precondition, but blast radius spans the full customer base

compliance_gaps.gap_description: Server-side validation exists for account ownership on the CRM tool, but refund amount and other tool-call parameters generated by the model are not independently re-validated against business rules before execution.
recommended_additional_controls: Add a business-rule validation layer for every tool-call parameter, independent of the model's own reasoning
Add a human-approval gate for refund actions above a defined threshold
Add automated adversarial test cases (prompt injection attempts) to the pre-release test suite

recommendations: Layer a dedicated input/output guardrail model in front of the agent to reduce (not eliminate) prompt injection risk (mitigates T-1, T-2).
Add an ingestion-time content sanitization step for all documents entering the vector store (mitigates T-2).
Require human approval for refund tool calls above a low threshold, and validate all tool-call parameters server-side (mitigates E-1, E-2, supports AC-1 mitigation).
Add append-only, access-controlled audit logging across prompts, completions, retrieved context, and tool calls (mitigates R-1, supports AC-1 detection).
Enforce hard tenant-scoped filtering at the RAG retrieval layer, not just a namespace convention (mitigates I-2).
Require signed model artifacts and version-tagged inference responses before any model reaches production (mitigates T-4, R-2, supports AC-2 detection).
Add training-data validation and provenance tracking to the fine-tuning pipeline (mitigates T-3, supports AC-2 mitigation).
references: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) -- genai.owasp.org
